coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly and sustainable synthesis of carbon quantum dots from waste sulfuric acid of alkylation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

coredata.dc:creator: Ling, Chen
Wang, Zhikun
Li, Chaolin
Wang, Wenhui
coredata.dc:description: Waste sulfuric acid of alkylation (WSAA) poses a challenge in industrial alkylation oil production due to its substantial yield and high treatment costs. This study introduces a novel hydrothermal method that ingeniously couples the treatment of WSAA with the synthesis of high-quality carbon quantum dots (CQDs), offering a cost-effective and controllable approach. Through this method, the particle size and surface functional groups of the resulting CQDs can be precisely regulated. The average particle size tunes from 17.97 nm to 2.42 nm via increasing the hydrothermal temperature, and nitrogen-containing groups can be introduced through adding nitrogen sources during hydrothermal process. The prepared CQDs exhibit notable performance in photocatalysis and heavy metal detection, such as CQDs modified graphite carbon nitride has improved photocatalytic degradation ability and Hg (II) detection ability. Additionally, the environmental impact of the proposed method is substantially minimized, demonstrating a smaller footprint on ecosystems compared to conventional industrial disposal processes. Moreover, the economic cost associated with the method is significantly reduced by approximately 48.4 %, further highlighting its efficiency. Utilizing WSAA as a raw material for CQDs not only facilitates the recycling and sustainable utilization of waste but also enables low-cost production of high-performance carbon materials, presenting a mutually beneficial approach.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Waste sulfuric acid of alkylation
Carbon quantum dots
Controllable preparation

The proposed method of CQDs production
Regulation of particle size and surface functional groups of CQDs-WSAA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process and characterization of CQDs-WSAA. (a) Synthesis scheme of CQDs-WSAA. (b) Fluorescence emission of CQDs-WSAA under UV-365 nm irradiation. (c) TEM image, (d) UV absorption spectrum, and (e) Photoluminescence spectra (PL) of CQDs-WSAA.
TEM images and particle size distribution of the CQDs-WSAA at 180 °C, 200 °C and 220 °C, respectively.
Regulation of functional groups of CQDs-WSAA. (a) FT-IR spectra and (b) PL of CQDs-WSAA and N-CQDs-WSAA.
Photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4/CQDs-WSAA (a) Degradation of CBZ. (b) Cycle performance test.

Mercury (II) Detection Using CQDs (a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs with different concentrations of Hg (II) under an excitation wavelength of 375 nm. (b) The linear correlation between the integral area and the concentration of Hg (II). (c) (F0 − F)/F0 of several common metal ions. F0 and F denote the integrated area of the fluorescence spectra of CQDs without and with the addition of metal ions, respectively.

Economic and environmental analysis. (a) Sustainability comparison of traditional and CQDs synthesis from WSAA disposal methods. (b) Comparative life-cycle climate change impacts of 1 t WSAA disposal. (c) Economic cost comparison for CQDs production.
Equipment investment for CQDs prepared from citric acid/WSAA by hydrothermal method.
Material cost for preparation of CQDs-WSAA by hydrothermal method.
Material cost for the preparation of CQDs from citric acid by hydrothermal method.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly and sustainable synthesis of carbon quantum dots from waste sulfuric acid of alkylation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment and Resources, Taiyuan University of Science and Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Taiyuan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment and Resources, Taiyuan University of Science and Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen, Shenzhen 518055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen, Shenzhen 518055, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150090, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150090, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Waste sulfuric acid of alkylation (WSAA) poses a challenge in industrial alkylation oil production due to its substantial yield and high treatment costs. This study introduces a novel hydrothermal method that ingeniously couples the treatment of WSAA with the synthesis of high-quality carbon quantum dots (CQDs), offering a cost-effective and controllable approach. Through this method, the particle size and surface functional groups of the resulting CQDs can be precisely regulated. The average particle size tunes from 17.97 nm to 2.42 nm via increasing the hydrothermal temperature, and nitrogen-containing groups can be introduced through adding nitrogen sources during hydrothermal process. The prepared CQDs exhibit notable performance in photocatalysis and heavy metal detection, such as CQDs modified graphite carbon nitride has improved photocatalytic degradation ability and Hg (II) detection ability. Additionally, the environmental impact of the proposed method is substantially minimized, demonstrating a smaller footprint on ecosystems compared to conventional industrial disposal processes. Moreover, the economic cost associated with the method is significantly reduced by approximately 48.4 %, further highlighting its efficiency. Utilizing WSAA as a raw material for CQDs not only facilitates the recycling and sustainable utilization of waste but also enables low-cost production of high-performance carbon materials, presenting a mutually beneficial approach.

ce:section.ce:para: Given the escalating demands for environmental protection and the accelerated advancement in gasoline quality standards, there is a growing need for alkylated oils characterized by high octane numbers and environmental friendliness [1–4]. This highlights the particular importance of alkylation technology. Among various alkylation techniques, sulfuric acid alkylation technology is relatively mature and widely utilized. This process utilizes concentrated sulfuric acid as an effective catalyst for alkylation reactions at temperatures of 8–12 °C. While, the concentrated sulfuric acid loses its catalytic activity and severely corrodes reaction equipment when its concentration falls below 85 %. At the result, the residual sulfuric acid hazardous waster manifests as a black viscous liquid termed waste sulfuric acid of alkylation (WSAA) is produced [5], which comprises around 90 % sulfuric acid, approximately 7 % organic matter, and roughly 3 % H2O. China's annual production capacity for WSAA exceeds 1 million tons [6,7]. To avoid environmental pollution, WSAA are treated by two primary methods. The first method involves high temperature incineration, which process oxidizes the organic matter in WSAA into CO2 and H2O, resulting in a significant amount of greenhouse gas emissions [8,9]. The second method is high temperature pyrolysis, where waste acid is used to produce SO2, which is then utilized as the raw gas to concentrate sulfuric acid. However, the corrosiveness of waste acid and pyrolysis gas leads to high equipment investment and operation costs, thereby reducing the economic feasibility [10,11]. In addition, there have been reports on the regeneration of WSAA using electrochemical methods [12], as well as the treatment of spent acidic propylene into isopropyl sulfate, followed by extraction using isobutene [13]. However, these methods suffer from low efficiency and high costs, making industrialization unattainable. Apparently, there is an urgent need to develop a more sustainable and promising recycling method.

On the flip side, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a zero-dimensional spherical carbon particles with extremely small size below 10 nm [14,15], have found widespread applications in environmental monitoring, chemical analysis, and medical imaging technology, due to its outstanding optical properties, good water solubility, and biocompatibility [16–18]. In general, CQDs can be synthesized through top-down approaches and bottom-up approaches. Among them, bottom-up methods, including ultrasonic synthesis [19], microwave-assisted synthesis [20], and hydrothermal route [21], stand out as effective technologies as they offer precision by adjusting synthesis parameters to control the properties, shape, and size of CQDs [22]. Nevertheless, bottom-up methods has several drawbacks: (1) it is environmentally harmful due to the need for organic solvents or strong oxidizing agents; (2) the raw materials are expensive, such as graphene and carbon nanotubes [23]. Coincidentally, WSAA, a hazardous waste, contains a substantial amount of sulfuric acid and organic compounds, making it an ideal raw material for CQD synthesis. WSAA not only acts as a rich carbon source for CQD synthesis but also eliminates the need for adding strong oxidizing agents, providing evident economic and environmental advantages over alternative CQD synthesis strategies.

Herein, we propose a novel approach to simultaneously treat WSAA and create economic value through waste utilization. The proposed strategy involves the hydrothermal method, which facilitates the rapid conversion of WSAA into functional CQDs (CQDs-WSAA), enabling the modulation of CQDs-WSAA particle sizes by adjusting reaction temperatures and introducing nitrogen-containing functional groups on the CQDs-WSAA surface through the incorporation of a nitrogen source during synthesis. The synthesized CQDs-WSAA exhibit promising environmental applications, such as photocatalytic degradation and Hg (II) detection. Furthermore, an economic assessment and life cycle analysis of this process underscore its significant application potential.

CQDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. Initially, 1 mL of WSAA was mixed with 1 mL of deionized water in a hydrothermal reactor featuring a stainless steel exterior and a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) lining with a volume of 100 mL. The reactor was then placed in a blast drying oven set at 180–220 °C for the hydrothermal reaction, proceeding for 3 h under autogenous pressure conditions. After removal from the oven and natural cooling to room temperature, the liquid from the reactor was transferred to a centrifuge tube, where the supernatant was collected through high-speed centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, filtration through a 0.22 μm membrane was carried out to eliminate larger particles from the supernatant. The pH of the supernatant was adjusted to 7.0 using sodium hydroxide, followed by dialysis using a 3500 Da membrane for 12 h. The dialyzed supernatant underwent freeze-drying to yield a powder comprising CQDs and sodium sulfate. Finally, due to the solubility of CQDs in ethanol and the insolubility of sodium sulfate in ethanol, high-purity CQDs were obtained through ethanol extraction.

The particle size distribution and morphology of CQDs were analyzed using the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI Tecnai G2 F30) and the projection electron microscope (FEI Spirit T12). The absorption spectrum of CQDs was measured with the Shimadzu UV–Visible Spectrophotometer UV-2450/2550. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CQDs, excited at 375 nm, were obtained using the highly sensitive integrated fluorescence spectrometer, FluoroMax-4 (HORIBA Scientific). Additionally, the surface functional groups of CQDs were characterized using the Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs composite g-C3N4 catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: In a magnetic stirrer, 2 g of melamine were dispersed in 60 mL of ethylene glycol, mixed thoroughly, followed by the addition of 45 mL of HNO3 (1:40). The mixture was stirred for 10 min to form a gel-like substance. The white gel produced was collected through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and placed in 50 mL of absolute ethanol. Subsequently, 1 mL (adding 100 uL per minute) of the required type of CQDs solution was added and the mixture was stirred for 30 min. The stirred solids were then collected using a 0.22 μm filter membrane, calcined at 550 °C for 4 h with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1. After natural cooling to room temperature, g-C3N4 modified by CQDs was obtained.

The synthesis of composite catalysts g-C3N4/CQDs-WSAA, g-C3N4/CQDs-CAE, and g-C3N4 differed in the type of CQDs solution added. (1) g-C3N4/CQDs-WSAA: CQDs were synthesized using waste sulfuric acid from alkylation. (2) g-C3N4/CQDs-CAE: CQDs were synthesized using citric acid and ethylenediamine. (3) g-C3N4: Catalyst without the addition of CQDs solution.

The reaction setup consisted of an LED light (wavelength range 400 nm-780 nm), threaded reagent vial, and magnetic stirrer. Prior to the reaction, the suspension solution was stirred under dim light conditions at 100 rpm for 1 h to ensure adsorption–desorption equilibrium of the photocatalyst with CBZ. In the photocatalytic degradation of CBZ, 100 µL of the reaction solution was withdrawn at predetermined intervals. The withdrawn 100 µL reaction liquid was thoroughly mixed with 1.9 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, transferred to a 1 mL vial for analysis.

The analysis of the target pollutant was performed by a liquid chromatography (H-class Plus, Waters, USA) equipped with a C18 column. The mobile phase consisted of deionized acetonitrile and a 0.5 ‰ acetic acid solution with a ratio of 40 % to 60 %, flowing at 1 mL min−1, with a UV detector wavelength set at 254 nm. Throughout all tests, the column temperature was maintained at 35 °C, and a sample volume of 10 µL was used. The removal efficiency of pollutants was calculated using Equation 1:
D e g r a d a t i o n r a t e
%
=
C 0
-
C t
/
C 0
× 100 %
where Ct and C0 represent the pollutant concentration at time t min of degradation and the initial state, respectively.

For the cyclic stability test of the composite photocatalyst, after the first round of photocatalytic degradation, the solution was filtered, the solid catalyst powder was collected, which was repeatedly cleaned with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C, and then the next round of photocatalytic degradation experiment was carried out.

A 100 mM Hg standard solution was prepared using Hg (NO3)2/H2O. Specifically, 0.162 g of Hg (NO3)2/H2O was dissolved in 10 mL of HNO3 (1/10). The CQDs solution was prepared and subsequently diluted tenfold with deionized water, designated as CQDs (1/10). To determine the concentration of Hg (II), 400 μL of CQDs (1/10) was utilized. Different concentrations (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, and 200 μM) of Hg (II) solutions were added to 400 μL of CQDs (1/10), and the Hg concentration was detected. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded under a fluorescence photometer, with an excitation wavelength set to 375 nm. Under identical conditions, 400 μL of CQDs (1/10) and 3 mL of standard solutions containing other metal ions (Al (III), Cu (II), Mg (II), Na (I), K (I), and Zn (II)) were added. The fluorescence spectrum of each metal ion was recorded using the fluorescence photometer (excitation wavelength: 375 nm).

The research focused on a local WSAA treatment plant, utilizing GREET 2020 for a comprehensive life cycle assessment [24]. The chosen functional unit was the disposal of 1 ton of WSAA. The conventional WSAA disposal involved the wet gas sulfuric acid regeneration process (WSA), aimed at recycling WSAA to produce industrial concentrated sulfuric acid. In contrast, our proposed method transforms WSAA into CQDs, as depicted in Fig. 5a. To holistically compare the environmental performance of the two approaches, the endpoint method of GREET was employed to assess the entire life cycle's impact. Inventory data were sourced from the Ecoinvent database (V.3.5), supplemented by data from this study and relevant literature. In evaluating the environmental impact, key metrics and indicators such as energy consumption, greenhouse gas emissions, water usage, and waste generation were meticulously analyzed. Through a detailed analysis of these factors, the study aimed to provide a comprehensive understanding of the environmental implications associated with both the conventional WSA method and the novel CQDs conversion process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The proposed method of CQDs production
ce:section.ce:para: In our economic assessment, the production process of unit CQDs solution (1 kg) served as the basis for calculation, allowing for a comparison with the prevailing production processes reported in current research.

Table 1 illustrates the preparation of CQDs from WSAA, incurring a total fixed investment of USD 10,584. With a lifespan of 5 years, 10 units operate simultaneously, yielding a production capacity of 74.67 g/h. The daily output of carbon quantum dots reaches 1.79 kg/day, operating for 300 days per year. The total depreciation cost of the equipment amounts to USD 3.94/kg.

Detailed analysis of the equipment's operating costs reveals the daily electricity consumption for producing CQDs. The total daily electricity consumption is 28.721 kWh, incurring an electricity cost of USD 4.64 and an operating cost of USD 2.60/kg (Table 2 ).

Materials consumed in the process include WSAA, tap water, and NaOH for pH neutralization (Table 3 ). The material cost for producing 1 kg of CQDs is USD -1.42. Taking all factors into account, the total production cost using WSAA as the raw material is USD 5.12/kg.

The equipment used in the traditional production process mirrors that of the CQDs-WSAA process, involving a total investment of USD 10,584. Operating for 5 years with 10 units running simultaneously, it yields a production capacity of 94.5 g/h, resulting in a daily output of 2.26 kg. Equipment depreciation cost is USD 3.09/kg, and the operating cost is USD 2.03/kg. Utilizing tartaric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials incurs a higher material cost at USD 4.81 /kg (Table 4 ). Considering all costs, the total production cost for 1 kg of CQDs is USD 9.93.

To verify the implementation of the strategy of using WSAA as a raw material for CQDs synthesis, we designed a fast and controllable method through hydrothermal reaction (i.e., 180 °C for 5 h in polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave), centrifugal filtration, dialysis and freezing drying (Fig. 1 a). Subsequently, the prepared CQDs-WSAA were characterized for their physical and chemical properties. As shown in Fig. 1b, the prepared CQDs-WSAA are well dispersed and the solution of CQDs-WSAA emits bright blue light under UV-365 nm irradiation, which preliminarily confirms the existence of CQDs and their fluorescence effect. As revealed by transmission electron microscope (TEM) image (Fig. 1c), the particle size of CQDs-WSAA is uniformly distributed between ∼ 1–6 nm [25,26]. Fig. 1d illustrates that the CQDs-WSAA synthesized exhibit robust absorption in the ultraviolet region (200–380 nm), a tailing phenomenon towards longer wavelengths, and distinct absorption peaks at 260 nm and 305 nm. The peak at 260 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the C=C and C–C bonds, which originated from the aromatic π system and the core sp2 carbon bonds [27]. The peak at 305 nm can be attributed to the n-π* transition of the C=O bond [28]. Fig. 1e indicates that the CQDs-WSAA exhibit the highest fluorescence intensity at a wavelength of 450 nm [29,30]. These above results collectively validate the successful synthesis of CQDs-WSAA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regulation of particle size and surface functional groups of CQDs-WSAA
ce:section.ce:para: To showcase the controllable synthesis of CQDs derived from WSAA, we independently adjusted particle size and functional groups, factors directly influencing the physical and chemical properties of CQDs [31,32]. Our observations revealed a consistent reduction in CQDs' particle size with an increase in hydrothermal temperature (Fig. 2 ). Specifically, the average particle size of the CQDs products decreased from 17.97 nm to 4.83 nm and 2.42 nm as the hydrothermal temperature rose from 180 °C to 200 °C and 220 °C, respectively.

Furthermore, functionalized CQDs-WSAA were readily synthesized by introducing appropriate sources. For instance, the addition of a nitrogen source (i.e., ammonia) led to the incorporation of nitrogen functionalities. As evident from the FT-IR spectra in Fig. 3 a, CQDs-WSAA exhibited predominant surface functionalization with C=O groups (1577 cm−1). In the case of N-CQDs-WSAA, additional FT-IR absorption peaks corresponding to NH2 groups (780 cm−1) and N–H (3070 cm−1) emerged, signifying the successful addition of nitrogen-containing groups onto the surface of CQDs-WSAA [33,34]. Surprisingly, as shown in Fig. 3b, the PL spectra of the N-CQDs-WSAA shows a red-shift with the excitation wavelength shifting from 450 to 420 nm, and N-CQDs-WSAA demonstrated a superior fluorescence intensity compared to CQDs-WSAA. This behavior may originate from the different surface states of the functional groups on the N-CQDs-WSAA, which could generate various surface emissive trap [35]. The above results emphasize the successful modulation of CQDs' characteristics through systematic adjustments, offering the potential for enhanced control in their synthesis.

Owing to its outstanding optical properties, minimal biological toxicity, and superior biocompatibility, CQDs have found applications in diverse fields, encompassing photocatalysis, metal instance detection, bio-imaging, and more [36]. To substantiate the assertion that the properties of CQDs-WSAA are as promising as those derived from commercial chemical carbon sources with high purities, we conducted a rigorous examination of their performance in photocatalysis and metal instance detection.

CQDs can facilitate the transfer of photo-generated electrons on the surface of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), thereby retarding the recombination of electron-hole pairs and enhancing its photocatalytic performance [37]. Consequently, we explored the promoting impact of CQD-WSAA on the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 under visible light. Through X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the C3N4 crystal structure before and after modification, it was revealed that the g-C3N4/CQDs retained the characteristic diffraction peaks of C3N4 (JCPDS 87-1526) post-modification. Noteworthy, there were no discernible alterations in the crystal structure, as illustrated in Fig. S3. This underscores the structural integrity and stability of g- C3N4/CQDs upon modification. Initially, carbamazepine (CBZ) demonstrated negligible degradation under visible light or in the presence of catalysts without visible light, as indicated by Fig. S1. In contrast, g-C3N4 doped with CQDs derived from WSAA (g-C3N4/CQDs-WSAA) exhibited a remarkable 97.1 % degradation of CBZ pollutants under visible light exposure for 1 h, a substantial improvement compared to pure g-C3N4 (∼78.7 %) (Fig. 4 a). Moreover, the catalytic efficacy of g-C3N4/CQDs-WSAA surpassed that of g-C3N4 doped with CQDs derived from citric acid and ethylenediamine (g-C3N4/CQDs-CAE). The CBZ degradation ability of g-C3N4/CQDs-WSAA was notably superior [38]. Following a 2 h degradation period, the total organic carbon (TOC) decreased from an initial concentration of 1.142 mg/L to 0.502 mg/L, resulting in a removal efficiency of 56.04 %, indicative of the excellent mineralization ability of g-C3N4/CQDs-WSAA (Fig. S2). Fig. 4b demonstrates the commendable cycle stability of g-C3N4/CQDs-CAE. After 5 rounds of photocatalytic degradation experiments, the degradation rate of CBZ is still greater than 97 %, which emphasizes its practical application potential.

CQDs find significant utility in the detection of heavy metals, a crucial application area that we have also explored. In our endeavors, CQDs-WSAA demonstrated its efficacy in detecting Hg (II), a highly toxic heavy metal implicated in severe health issues [35]. As depicted in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-WSAA gradually diminishes with increasing concentrations of Hg (II), owing to its fluorescence quenching effect. Notably, the quenching ratio ((F0 − F)/F0) exhibits a linear correlation with Hg (II) concentration, achieving a high R2 value of 0.973 (Fig. 5b). Particularly noteworthy is the robust tolerance of the Hg (II) detection system to the interference of common metal ions such as Na (I), K (I), Ca (II), Mg (II), Cu (II), Zn (II), and Al (III) at a concentration of 150 μM. The fluorescence intensity reduction induced by these metal ions is < 9.6 %, deemed acceptable when compared to the 52.5 % fluorescence quenching observed with Hg (II) (Fig. 5c). The specificity of CQDs-WSAA for Hg (II) detection rivals that of Au and N co-doped CQDs, whose fluorescence intensity induced by other metal ions in water decreased is less than 10 %, as reported in the state-of-art literatures [39].

In revolutionizing the production of CQDs, our developed method stands out with multiple advantages over traditional approaches. Foremost, the utilization of WSAA as the raw material not only achieves waste-to-resource conversion but also breaks free from the constraints of high raw material costs inherent in conventional methods. This breakthrough not only propels sustainable CQDs production but also opens doors for their widespread application on a larger scale. Secondly, the pyrolysis of WSAA proves to be more efficient compared to the high-purity carbon sources typically used in conventional methods. The hydrothermal reaction process for CQDs conversion takes a mere 3 h, significantly shorter than the conventional 5 h duration. This not only expedites the production process but also substantially reduces energy consumption, contributing to a more sustainable and efficient approach. Crucially, our method introduces a pivotal economic and environmental advantage by eliminating the necessity for additional strong oxidizing agents. This strategic choice enhances the cost-effectiveness of the process and aligns with environmentally conscious practices. The elimination of such agents underscores the commitment to an eco-friendly production process. In conclusion, the CQDs-WSAA produced through our method offers not only versatile applications but also exhibits exceptional performance. This includes applications in photocatalysis and metal instance detection, showcasing the potential and versatility of this innovative production methodology.

In the pursuit of sustainable practices, the treatment of WSAA was ingeniously coupled with the synthesis of CQDs. Recognizing the potential environmental and economic advantages inherent in this approach, a comprehensive comparative analysis was conducted against the prevalent industrial method. This approach champions the resource utilization of WSAA and demonstrates a smaller environmental footprint compared to industrial disposal practices. As shown in Fig. 6
a, the traditional WSAA disposal process exhibits higher carbon emissions compared to the treatment method used. Life cycle assessment results, as depicted in Fig. 6b, underscore the reduced impact on global warming, reflected in the lower CO2 equivalent. Traditional CQDs preparation methods often rely on non-renewable carbon precursors like citric acid and ethylenediamine, incurring substantial costs and energy consumption. In stark contrast, the approach illustrated in Fig. 6a harnesses the abundant carbon reservoir within WSAA as a primary carbon precursor for CQDs production. This strategic shift dramatically reduces costs and energy consumption, aligning the methodology with resource efficiency and conservation. The utilization of WSAA not only facilitates low-cost CQDs production but also aligns with sustainable practices. The estimated economic cost of the preparation process is USD 5.12, representing a noteworthy reduction of approximately 48.4 % compared to the traditional CQDs preparation cost, which stands at USD 9.93 (Fig. 6c). This substantial improvement underscores the significant cost-effectiveness achieved through the innovative method.

In conclusion, this study introduces an environmentally conscious approach to fabricating CQDs utilizing WSAA as a precursor. The resultant CQDs exhibit promising capabilities in enhancing photocatalysis and detecting heavy metals. Notably, this strategy not only ensures a cost-effective CQDs synthesis but also mitigates carbon emissions linked to WSAA treatment. The research charts a new course for the sustainable utilization of WSAA resources, presenting an innovative avenue for the secure, cost-efficient, and eco-friendly production of CQDs. However, the scalability of the proposed synthesis process and the long-term stability of the CQDs in various applications remain to be thoroughly investigated. Future research should focus on optimizing the synthesis parameters, pilot-scale synthesis and exploring the potential of CQDs in diverse environmental and industrial applications. Overall, it constitutes a significant stride toward meeting the dual objectives of curbing peak carbon emissions and achieving carbon neutrality, holding encouraging prospects for addressing environmental challenges in the field.

Chen Ling: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Zhikun Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Chaolin Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wenhui Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52170155 and 52100084).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2024.08.050.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

Y. Wang, J. Shi, Z. Jin, Z. Xie, Chinese Journal of Catalysis, 39 (2018) 1147-1156.
Z. Zhou, X. Zhang, F. Yang, S. Zhang, Journal of Cleaner Production, 215 (2019) 13-21.
S. van Ewijk, J.A. Stegemann, P. Ekins, Nature Sustainability, 4 (2021) 180.
Y. Zhang, T. Gan, H. Hu, X. Cai, Z. Huang, X. Liang, Y. Yin, Y. Qin, Z. Feng, Journal of Hazardous Materials, 380 (2019).
J. Dai, X. Yang, B. Wang, Z. Chen, Z. Lu, Z. Bai, Journal of Cleaner Production, 276 (2020).
P.A. Sorensen, M. Mollerhoj, K.A. Christensen, Chemical Engineering Journal, 278 (2015) 421-429.
